programming_answers:
SELECT department, COUNT(*) as headcount, AVG(salary) as avg_salary
FROM employees
WHERE status = 'active'
GROUP BY department
HAVING COUNT(*) > 5
ORDER BY avg_salary DESC;

SELECT c.customer_name, SUM(o.order_value) as total_spent,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(o.order_value) DESC) as spending_rank
FROM customers c
JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.order_date >= DATEADD(month, -12, GETDATE())
GROUP BY c.customer_name;
session1_transcript:
[00:00:15] Interviewer_Technical_Senior: Welcome Omar. Walk us through how you approach gathering requirements for a new internal system.
[00:00:35] Candidate: I start by identifying all stakeholders — not just the sponsor but end users, IT, compliance, and any upstream or downstream system owners. I run structured discovery workshops where I ask stakeholders to describe current pain points, desired outcomes, and constraints separately before consolidating. I use MoSCoW prioritization to separate must-haves from nice-to-haves and document everything as user stories with acceptance criteria so developers have unambiguous definitions of done.
[00:01:10] Interviewer_Technical_Junior: How do you handle conflicting requirements from different stakeholders?
[00:01:25] Candidate: Conflicts are usually about unstated assumptions rather than genuine disagreements. I bring the conflicting parties together and ask each to explain the business outcome they are trying to achieve rather than the feature they want. Usually the outcomes are compatible and we can find a solution that satisfies both. If there is a genuine conflict I escalate to the project sponsor with a clear options analysis showing the tradeoffs of each path.
[00:02:10] Interviewer_Technical_Senior: Can you describe a time you used data analysis to drive a business recommendation?
[00:02:30] Candidate: In my previous role I noticed customer churn was increasing. I pulled transaction data and built a cohort analysis in Excel showing that customers who had not made a second purchase within 30 days had an 80 percent churn rate. I presented this to the marketing team with a recommendation to trigger an automated re-engagement email at day 20. After implementation churn in that segment dropped by 35 percent over three months.
[00:03:15] Interviewer_Technical_Junior: How comfortable are you with SQL and what kinds of queries do you write regularly?
[00:03:40] Candidate: Very comfortable. I write complex aggregations with GROUP BY and HAVING clauses, window functions like RANK and LAG for trend analysis, and multi-table joins. I also write CTEs to break complex queries into readable steps. I profile query performance and add indexes where needed — I once reduced a dashboard query from 45 seconds to under 2 seconds by adding a composite index on the most common filter columns.
session2_transcript:
[00:00:10] Interviewer_HR: What would you say is your greatest professional strength?
[00:00:25] Candidate: Translating between technical and non-technical stakeholders. I can sit with a developer and discuss data models and then walk into a boardroom and explain the same system in terms of business outcomes and risk. I find that most project failures I have seen come from communication gaps rather than technical ones.
[00:01:10] Interviewer_HR: Describe a situation where you had to deliver difficult news to a stakeholder.
[00:01:30] Candidate: A project I was leading was going to miss its deadline because the scope had grown significantly after sign-off. Instead of waiting until the deadline I called the sponsor three weeks early, brought a clear analysis of what was added to scope and why it caused the delay, and presented three options with their tradeoffs. The sponsor chose to descope two features and we delivered on time. They told me later they appreciated the early warning and the structured options rather than just being told bad news.
[00:02:15] Interviewer_HR: Where do you see your career going over the next few years?
[00:02:30] Candidate: I want to move into a senior BA or business systems lead role where I am responsible for a portfolio of improvement initiatives rather than individual projects. I am also interested in developing deeper data skills — I am currently studying for my Power BI certification — so I can be more self-sufficient in the analysis work rather than always depending on a data analyst.
